chapters.title: Why biodiversity is vital to people's existence
chapters.text:
Seventy thousand years ago, the survival of the human species came under threat. The freezing climate, dramatic temperature changes, and difficulty finding fresh water were hard to bear. Only 20,000 people may have remained on the whole planet then. Humanity needed stability to thrive, and the Holocene gave them this opportunity. This geological epoch started 11,500 years ago and brought unthinkable balance. The average temperature hadn't changed for almost 100 centuries.

Scientists still debate what caused it, but biodiversity played a pivotal role. Phytoplankton in oceans and woodland reduced the level of carbon dioxide through photosynthesis. Rainforests enriched the air with moisture while many insects, birds, bats, and small mammals pollinated the plants, sustaining their existence. Together they moderated the planet's temperature. Humans survived due to this teamwork, yet now they put it at risk.

Everything is interconnected in natural ecosystems, which makes them strong yet vulnerable. One change can undermine their stability.

“What impact can the disappearance of one species have? Is it so critical?” some ask nowadays. Rainforests, home to over 50% of land-living species, can answer these questions. Having little space and limited resources, their inhabitants became specialized. For instance, some beetles live on one type of herb, which grows only near one tree species. Eliminating one part of this chain will destabilize the whole system, and that's what happens now.

Roughly half of the rainforests and a quarter of insect species are gone. In the last seventy years, populations of wild animals decreased by 50%. So, those who helped humanity overcome the crisis thousands of years ago have weakened now. Yet the upcoming challenge only gets stronger.

Join Sir David Attenborough on his journey into wild nature, exploring the measures that can save the planet and humanity.

chapters.title: How Sir David Attenborough has innovated natural history programs
chapters.text:
As a child, the future broadcaster spent hours exploring everything from fossils to hazelnut shells in the countryside near his hometown. He always dreamed of visiting distant places hardly anyone had seen, and in adulthood, he found an opportunity to do that.

There were only two small studios when he started his job at the BBC. Programs about natural history were in their infancy at that time. Journalists used to take animals from the local zoo, put them on the table in front of a camera, and explain their peculiarities. Many felt the setup seemed contrived and lacked authenticity.

Sir David Attenborough, by contrast, wanted to show the audience animals in their natural habitat. So, he and a cameraperson went to Sierra Leone with Jack Lester, the curator of the reptile house at London Zoo. They planned to show a film about the trip on TV, and Lester would appear in the studio, discussing natural history. Tragically, he became ill after the first episode and was unable to continue the following week. As a result, despite his original role as producer, Sir David Attenborough found himself narrating the show, beginning his journey as one of the most famous nature broadcasters.

Discoveries are made by those who choose demanding paths.

He challenged himself with ambitious goals from the beginning. At that time, most explorers went through wild nature by jeeps or helicopters. Yet it was too easy for Sir David Attenborough. He chose a place where traveling by foot was the only option — New Guinea. Some of its parts were complete mysteries. Strangers had never entered them, and aerial photographs couldn't tell whether people lived there.

In 1971, officials decided to seek answers and sent an expedition, which Sir David Attenborough joined. It was an exhausting trip — dense forest, drenching rains, and no signs of human life for more than three weeks. But suddenly, they noticed footprints and understood that someone was watching them. When Sir David Attenborough woke up the following day, he saw meter-and-a-half-tall men. Gesture language helped them to communicate. As a result, the programs obtained new dimensions — people's stories and customs.

His following projects were even more ambitious. While filming Life on Earth, Sir David Attenborough visited over 25 countries to show viewers 600 species. The intention behind this series was wider than creating exciting TV content. He also has another essential goal — awakening humanity to the consequences of its actions.

chapters.title: Television amplified nature's call for help
chapters.text:
For years, humanity was largely oblivious to its environmental impact. A few reasons can explain it. Firstly, changes in nature resemble icebergs. Many processes are barely visible; only minor details, like warning flags, signal something is wrong. For instance, for a long time, people didn't realize the scale of the greenhouse effect. That's because the oceans absorbed immense amounts of heat, and air temperature remained stable.

Only coral reefs sent alarms few people could understand. Heeding their call, Sir David Attenborough filmed how they turned white. In a normal state, living inside a coral polyp, algae enriches it with photosynthesis energy and gives it specific colors. Warmer water makes polyps expel the algae and whiten, becoming more vulnerable to starvation.

Home to numerous species, coral reefs are the oceans' rainforests.

Secondly, society was often uninformed of the tragedies faced by wild nature. That's when the role of cameras in raising awareness became extremely important. For example, American biologist, Dian Fossey, strived to protect mountain gorillas in Rwanda, which were on the brink of extinction. Featuring their story in Life on Earth's episode, Sir David Attenborough helped raise money to sustain the project. He knew the camera's power well, as it significantly contributed to saving the biggest mammals on the planet — whales.

These animals, whose antecedents were land-living creatures, are essential for the ocean's biological productivity. Why? Nutrients sink to the seabed, leaving phytoplankton, the fundamental species for all other marine animals, without vital elements. Whales came to their help. Swimming from the surface to the deep and vice versa, they stir the water and move the nutrients from the depth to the top. Yet in the 20th century, people disrupted this balance, killing almost 3 million whales. They struggled to revive their population.

Yet, one coincidence helped them. American listening stations, placed to detect the sounds of Soviet submarines, heard unbelievable songs. Biologist Roger Payne recognized them as the serenades of humpback whales. He recorded and released them on vinyl disks. It was a turning point: the public started to consider whales as intelligent creatures with musical culture, not a source of oil. The basis for the song was the same, but each male came up with unique variations. This new take on the mammal’s image found many supporters. Some even managed to film the ruthless whaling industry, which led to massive social protests against it. The whales' serenades were SOS signals heard loud and clear.

chapters.title: How many pillars of the ecosystem's stability remain unbroken?
chapters.text:
Nowadays, holding enough data, scientists have estimated people's influence. Here are some of their findings:

• Annually, 15 billion trees disappear due to human activity.
• The rate of biodiversity loss is unprecedented. It exceeds the average by 100 times.
• Plastic in the water spreads to all levels of the food chain; over 85% of sea birds consume it with fish.
• The wealthiest 16% of the population account for almost half of the damage to the planet.

We moved from being a part of nature to being apart from nature. ~ Sir David Attenborough

Johan Rockström and Will Steffen's research group used data to discover critical thresholds, crossing which would make ecosystems fail. They singled out nine, calling them planetary boundaries:

• Climate change
• Biodiversity loss
• Land conversion
• Fertilizer use
• Ocean acidification
• Chemical pollution
• Air pollution
• Ozone layer depletion
• Freshwater withdrawals

From now on, people should evaluate their success by how much nature they have restored.

Humanity has already crossed the first four boundaries and is reaching the fifth one. If it continues to move in the same direction, the following predictions may come true:

• The 2030s: 25% of rainforest will remain. This reduction might trigger a drop in moisture, complicating the formation of clouds over the Amazon basin. It may disrupt the rainforest’s balance: some territories will be dry, while others will suffer from flooding. Wildfires will release immense carbon emissions, which trees have locked up. Additionally, the Arctic Ocean will be completely ice-free in the summer, affecting food chains. It will also result in a temperature rise as nothing will reflect the sunlight into space.
• The 2040s: Climate change can influence permafrost, ground that remains completely frozen for years. This warming will lead to floods, new river courses, landslides, and emissions. Permafrost caged a vast amount of carbon — 400% more than people released during the last two centuries.
• The 2050s: Warmer water will make 90% of coral reefs disappear.
• The 2080s: A decrease in pollinating insects may negatively impact 75% of food crops. Global food production will be in dire straits.
• The 2100s: Many cities will be underwater as sea levels rise, while 25% of the population will experience weather conditions akin to the Sahara today. Consequently, forced human migration could become common, increasing tensions between nations.

These scenarios demonstrate that humanity desperately needs to rethink its priorities. Initiatives that preserve the wild and promote biodiversity must become mainstream.

chapters.title: An ocean-centric approach to climate recovery
chapters.text:
Planetary boundaries may sound like a sentence for humanity, but the truth is they are guidelines for those ready to change. They help to focus all efforts on the most pressing issues and show what areas people should monitor to prevent crossing the thresholds.

Continents constitute only 3% of inhabitable space. The remaining 97% is in the oceans.

Some measures can help deal with a few challenges at once. For example, forest restoration will decrease the greenhouse effect, protect thousands of species, and provide people with food. Another measure is saving oceans. They constitute 70% of the Earth's surface and are essential for biodiversity and decreasing the carbon footprint. The following are some practical steps humanity should apply.

Reduce over-fishing. Half a century ago, only four ocean areas were rich in fish. Nowadays, the situation may be even worse, so introducing no-fishing zones is vital. Scientific data proved that leaving 33% of the ocean alone will recover marine ecosystems. Ideally, the chosen territories will be coral reefs, seagrass meadows, and other water inhabitants' favorite places. Sir David Attenborough considers this measure a deposit with a massive interest rate. Many organizations now work on new principles of fishing. Hopefully, long-term thinking and societal pressure will help them to make the right choice.

Rethink fish farms. People have destroyed a third of mangroves and seagrass beds worldwide for aquaculture. Additionally, packing farms with prawns and fish increases the risks of diseases. It leads to immense usage of antibiotics and disinfectants, which get into food and spread into the ocean. However, some farms are sustainable. Their locations are miles offshore, and fish density is much lower. Enriching the farms with sea cucumbers and urchins allows owners to reduce waste. Other entrepreneurs should follow these principles.

Popularize ocean foresters. Seaweed excels in capturing carbon and is a multipurpose “crop.” For instance, kelp, a large brown algae, is a food with high nutritional value and a source of bioenergy.

It is no accident that the planet's stability has wavered just as its biodiversity has declined – the two things are bound together. To restore stability to our planet, therefore, we must restore its biodiversity. ~ Sir David Attenborough

chapters.title: Earth's dietary plan — slimming down meat and fuel consumption
chapters.text:
Support for existing initiatives centered around clean energy and sustainable land usage is crucial. Releasing carbon at the same rate as now, people will reach the planetary boundary by the decade's end. Renewable power plants can resolve this problem, but now they produce only 4% of electricity worldwide. Indeed, their implementation still poses some challenges, like storage of this energy. At the same time, the cost of renewables fell significantly; soon, it will equal gas and oil.

According to scientists, paying $50 for each tonne of emissions will prompt the industry to switch from fossil fuels.

However, some crucial challenges aren't engineering but vested interests and inertia. The list of the ten world's largest companies features six specializing in fossil fuels. Also, most heavy industries get used to this energy source and want to keep their business model the same. Moreover, many investments in fossil fuels come from central banks and pension funds. So, governments should use their political will to undermine the current status quo. Formats of support may differ:

• Giving privileges to those who produce renewable energy
• Incentivizing people to establish micro-grids
• Introducing carbon tax

The latter has demonstrated incredible results; for instance, Sweden encouraged the industry to choose renewables using this method. Other examples also prove clean energy is an achievable task. Three countries have rejected fossil fuels: Albania, Paraguay, and Iceland. Eight more have ensured this source accounts for less than 10% of their electricity.

Another issue is to make farms more sustainable. Nowadays, they occupy over 50% of habitable land at the expense of wild nature. There are at least two ways to deal with this challenge:

• Changing consumer preferences. People spend over 75% of farmlands on meat and dairy production, including soy for animal feed. Therefore, lessening the consumption of these products will benefit nature enormously. Fortunately, this tendency now gains popularity.
• Improving agriculture. The low-cost way is regenerative farming. It entails rotating crops, decreasing the amount of fertilizers, and other measures. A more expensive solution is introducing innovations. For instance, farmers may apply climate-control systems and use nutrient-rich water instead of soil in greenhouses.

Did you know? The average American eats over 100 kg of meat annually, while each Peruvian consumes 24 kg.

chapters.title: The “win-win” model starts with new habits
chapters.text: Among all the changes people should introduce, one is indispensable — a new outlook. Prioritizing gross domestic product growth (GDP) led society to the Great Acceleration, which started in the 1950s. Since then, there has been a massive increase in consumption of Earth's resources. It gave people incredible opportunities and made life much easier. Yet nowadays, nature brings people the bill, and the current environmental crisis is the cost. Thus, it's time for humanity to find a new “win-win” model, balancing its needs and Earth's. What is the alternative? The first step is to replace the reliance on GDP as the primary indicator of a country's development. It might sound too challenging, yet some have risen to it. For instance, officials in New Zealand devised a new index in 2019. Reflecting the country's priorities, it rests on three principles — people, planet, and profit. It's one of the ways society can put nature at the center of its choices. Other potential avenues for adopting a nature-centered approach include the following: • Choosing commodities made in an environmentally friendly way. • Revolutionizing the design of products. Manufacturers should make long-lasting items and adjust commodities for a circular economy. This way, users can substitute some parts and recycle them. • Investing in sustainable business and green initiatives. • Encouraging the brightest minds to work on projects that aren't lucrative in the short-term but will make a change regarding human footprint. The phrase throw away has a pitfall. Everyone lives on the same planet, so away is impossible to implement. The nature-centered approach entails changing waste management practices. People should consider it a resource, not a problem. As in natural ecosystems, the waste of some organisms becomes a basis for others. There are many ways in which garbage can be helpful: • Plant and animal biomass by-products may become biochar, which acts like a sponge and benefits soils. People might also use it as building material or fuel with low emissions. • Some waste, like wood scraps, can be converted into bioenergy. • “Imperfect products,” discarded by cafes, supermarkets, and restaurants, can be sold at lower prices or sent to farms as feed for livestock. Did you know? People dispose of approximately 33% of food worldwide.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Human beings are remarkable in their ability to evolve constantly, achieving previously unattainable goals. At the same time, as Sir David Attenborough highlights, society genuinely thrives when it matures. It means longing for what is truly necessary, not for more, and relying on wisdom, not only intelligence.

The excellent news is that humanity has already leaped toward seeing itself as a part of nature. Initiatives at different levels work to ensure redressing the balance. For instance, Morocco is a glimmer of hope regarding clean energy. All its electricity stemmed from imported fossil fuels at the turn of the century. Nowadays, its renewable power plants produce almost half of the necessary amount. Moreover, Morocco has the biggest solar farm, and it may soon become a leading exporter of solar energy.

Another example is turning megalopolises into cities within a garden. While Singapore constructs buildings with places for plants and increases green zones, London officials aim to transform 50% of the city's area into natural spaces.

Tiger reserves in India, whale sanctuaries, numerous public campaigns, and other steps indicate that humanity has finally found the correct path. The key is not to roam away from it.

Try this


• Research the origin, material, durability, and disposal of commodities you will buy. Try to purchase eco-friendly options.
• Consider reducing your meat and dairy consumption.
• Be creative while planning your holidays. Choose new itineraries that require going by train or, ideally, bicycle. Use planes as little as possible.
• Spend resources wisely: take a shower, not a bath, prefer energy-efficient appliances, and reduce waste.
• Buy local products wherever possible. This way, you will lessen emissions spent on transportation.
